title: React Junior Developer Mock Test
description: Foundational assessment for junior frontend roles covering JSX, Hooks, Props vs State, Component Lifecycle, and Virtual DOM concepts.

questions.text: What is the primary purpose of the Virtual DOM in React?
options: To directly manipulate the browser's DOM for speed
To provide a blueprint for the UI that minimizes expensive DOM updates
To replace HTML with JavaScript entirely
To store data in the browser's local storage
questions.explanation: React uses the Virtual DOM to compare changes (diffing) and only update the specific parts of the real DOM that changed, improving performance.

questions.text: Which hook is used to handle side effects like API calls or subscriptions?
questions.explanation: The `useEffect` hook allows you to perform side effects in functional components, running after the render.

questions.text: How do you pass data from a parent component to a child component?
questions.explanation: Props (properties) are the standard way to pass data down the component tree.

questions.text: What will happen if you update a component's state directly (e.g., `this.state.count = 1`)?
options: The UI will update but the console will show a warning
React will throw a syntax error
questions.explanation: State must be updated via `setState` or a state setter from `useState` to trigger React's reconciliation process and re-render the UI.

questions.text: In JSX, how do you embed a JavaScript expression?
questions.explanation: Curly braces `{}` are used to evaluate JavaScript expressions inside JSX.

questions.text: What is the 'key' prop used for when rendering a list of elements?
options: To help React identify which items have changed, been added, or removed
To set the ID of the HTML element
To bind a click event to the item
questions.explanation: Keys provide a stable identity to elements in a list, allowing React to re-order or update them efficiently without re-rendering the entire list.

questions.text: Which of the following is a 'Rule of Hooks'?
options: Hooks must be called inside loops
Hooks can be called in nested functions
Hooks must only be called at the top level of a functional component
Hooks must be called in Class components
questions.explanation: Hooks must be called at the top level to ensure they are called in the same order every time a component renders.

options: A function to update the state only
An array containing the current state and a setter function
An object with all state variables
questions.explanation: `useState` returns a pair: the current state value and a function that lets you update it.

questions.text: How can you prevent a default action (like a form submission) in a React event handler?
questions.explanation: Just like in standard DOM, `event.preventDefault()` is used to prevent default browser behavior.

options: A way to group multiple elements without adding extra nodes to the DOM
A legacy way of writing components
questions.explanation: Fragments (`<React.Fragment>` or `<>`) let you group a list of children without adding extra `div` tags to the HTML output.

questions.text: What is the correct syntax for an arrow function component?
questions.explanation: Functional components can be defined as standard functions or arrow functions that return JSX.

questions.text: What happens if the dependency array of a `useEffect` hook is empty `[]`?
options: The effect runs on every render
The effect runs only once after the initial mount
The effect runs only when the component unmounts
questions.explanation: An empty dependency array tells React that the effect doesn't depend on any values from props or state, so it only runs once.

questions.text: Which command is commonly used to create a new React project using a modern build tool?
questions.explanation: `npx create-react-app` (or Vite's `npm create vite@latest`) are the standard ways to bootstrap a new React application.

questions.text: True or False: React components must start with a capital letter.
questions.explanation: React treats components starting with lowercase letters as DOM tags (like `<div>`). Custom components must be capitalized.

questions.text: What is 'lifting state up' in React?
options: Moving state to a global Redux store
Moving state to a common parent to share it between sibling components
Increasing the performance of a component
questions.explanation: Lifting state up involves moving shared state to the closest common ancestor so both child components can access it via props.
